content:
# Judges Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_272.jpg, JDG_page_273.jpg

## The Song of Deborah

1. Then Deborah and Barak the son of Abinoam sang on that day, saying:

2. That the leaders led in Israel,
that the people offered themselves willingly —
bless the LORD!

3. Hear, O kings; give ear, O rulers!
I, to the LORD, I will sing;
I will make melody to the LORD, the God of Israel.

4. LORD, when you went out from Seir,
when you marched from the region of Edom,
the earth trembled and the heavens poured,
the clouds also poured water.

5. The mountains quaked before the LORD —
even Sinai before the LORD, the God of Israel.

6. In the days of Shamgar son of Anath,
in the days of Jael, the highways were abandoned,
and travelers kept to the byways.

7. The villagers ceased in Israel; they ceased
until you arose, Deborah,
arose as a mother in Israel.

8. When new gods were chosen,
then war was in the gates.
Was shield or spear to be seen
among forty thousand in Israel?

9. My heart goes out to the commanders of Israel
who offered themselves willingly among the people.
Bless the LORD!

10. Tell of it, you who ride on white donkeys,
you who sit on rich saddle blankets,
and you who walk along the road.

11. To the sound of musicians at the watering places,
there they repeat the righteous triumphs of the LORD,
the righteous triumphs of his villagers in Israel.
Then down to the gates marched the people of the LORD.

12. Awake, awake, Deborah!
Awake, awake, break out in song!
Arise, Barak, lead away your captives, O son of Abinoam!

13. Then down marched the remnant of the nobles;
the people of the LORD marched down for me against the mighty.

14. From Ephraim their root was in Amalek;
after you, Benjamin, with your peoples;
from Machir marched down the commanders,
and from Zebulun those who bear the officer's staff.

15. The princes of Issachar came with Deborah,
and Issachar faithful to Barak;
into the valley they rushed at his heels.
Among the clans of Reuben
there were great searchings of heart.

16. Why did you sit among the sheepfolds,
to hear the whistling for the flocks?
Among the clans of Reuben
there were great searchings of heart.

17. Gilead stayed beyond the Jordan;
and Dan, why did he linger by the ships?
Asher sat still at the coast of the sea,
staying by his landings.

18. Zebulun is a people who risked their lives to the death;
Naphtali, too, on the heights of the field.

19. The kings came, they fought;
then fought the kings of Canaan,
at Taanach, by the waters of Megiddo;
they got no spoils of silver.

20. From heaven the stars fought,
from their courses they fought against Sisera.

21. The torrent Kishon swept them away,
the ancient torrent, the torrent Kishon.
March on, my soul, with might!

22. Then loud beat the horses' hoofs
with the galloping, galloping of his steeds.

23. "Curse Meroz," says the angel of the LORD,
"curse its inhabitants utterly,
because they did not come to the help of the LORD,
to the help of the LORD against the mighty."

24. Most blessed of women is Jael,
the wife of Heber the Kenite,
of tent-dwelling women most blessed.

25. He asked for water and she gave him milk;
she brought him curds in a noble's bowl.

26. She put her hand to the tent peg
and her right hand to the workmen's mallet.
She struck Sisera; she crushed his head;
she shattered and pierced his temple.

27. Between her feet he sank, he fell, he lay still;
between her feet he sank, he fell;
where he sank, there he fell — dead.

28. Out of the window she peered,
the mother of Sisera wailed through the lattice:
"Why is his chariot so long in coming?
Why tarry the hoofbeats of his chariots?"

29. Her wisest princesses answer,
indeed, she answers herself:

30. "Are they not finding and dividing the spoil? —
A womb or two for every warrior;
spoil of dyed fabric for Sisera,
spoil of dyed fabric, embroidered,
two pieces of dyed work embroidered for the neck as spoil."

31. So may all your enemies perish, O LORD!
But may those who love him be like the sun when it rises in its might.

And the land had rest for forty years.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The Song of Deborah is one of the crown jewels of Hebrew poetry — possibly the oldest extended poem in the Bible, with archaic grammatical forms, rare vocabulary, and several hapax legomena. Its passionate, visceral imagery stands in sharp contrast to the restrained prose of chapter 4. The poem moves from theophany (vv.4-5) through tribal roll call (vv.14-18) to the graphic death of Sisera (vv.26-27) and the devastating portrait of his waiting mother (vv.28-30).

**Verse 2 — The opening phrase:** biphroa p'ra'ot is one of the most debated expressions in the Hebrew Bible. The consensus follows the reading "that the leaders led" (from para', "to lead"), which creates a better parallel with "the people offered themselves willingly." EMET's "when locks of hair hung loose" (a warrior-vow interpretation) is a legitimate alternative.

**Verse 30 — racham rachamatayim:** Literally "a womb, two wombs." This is the poem's darkest line. Sisera's mother, waiting at the window, imagines her son delayed because he is distributing captured women — referred to by the crudest possible synecdoche. The consensus retains the literal "womb" rather than PNEUMA's "woman" because the dehumanizing language is the entire point.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.2 — "the leaders led" vs. "when locks of hair hung loose" vs. "when Israel's leaders step forward"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "When locks of hair hung loose in Yisrael" | p'ra'ot as "locks of hair" — warrior-vow reading |
| LOGOS | "That the leaders led in Israel" | p'ra'ot as "leaders" — from para', "to lead" |
| PNEUMA | "When Israel's leaders step forward" | Same root as LOGOS, dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The phrase biphroa p'ra'ot b'Yisrael is genuinely ambiguous — one of the most discussed in the Hebrew Bible. Three main interpretations: (a) "when locks were loosed" — warriors letting hair grow wild as a battle dedication (cf. Numbers 6, the Nazirite vow); (b) "when leaders led" — from para' meaning "to be at the head"; (c) "when anarchy prevailed" — from para' meaning "to be unbridled/lawless." The parallel with "the people offered themselves willingly" favors (b): leaders leading + people volunteering. EMET's (a) is noted as a strong alternative with deep roots in the tradition.

**Status:** Major. Fundamental ambiguity with no scholarly consensus.

---

### v.7 — "The villagers ceased" vs. "There were no warriors"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The villages ceased in Yisrael, they ceased" | p'razon — "villages/open country" |
| LOGOS | "The villagers ceased in Israel; they ceased" | Same root, "villagers" |
| PNEUMA | "There were no warriors in Israel" | Interprets p'razon as "warriors" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew p'razon can mean "villagers," "open country," or (less commonly) "warriors/leaders." In context, the verse describes the cessation of normal rural life — highways abandoned (v.6), villages deserted (v.7) — until Deborah arose. PNEUMA's "warriors" disrupts this picture of civilian collapse and imports a military reading not supported by the immediate context.

**Status:** Moderate. Affects the characterization of pre-Deborah Israel.

---

### v.7 — "until I arose" vs. "until you arose"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "until I arose, Devorah" | shaqamti — 1st person, "I arose" |
| LOGOS | "until you arose, Deborah" | Some MSS read 2nd person |
| PNEUMA | "not until I, Deborah, arose" | 1st person |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS (2nd person). The Masoretic qere reads shamti (2nd person "you arose") while the ketiv reads shaqamti (1st person "I arose"). The qere/ketiv distinction is preserved in the Leningrad Codex. Since SOPHIA follows the qere reading as standard Masoretic practice, the 2nd person "you arose" is adopted. EMET's first-person reading follows the ketiv and is noted.

**Status:** Moderate. Qere/ketiv variant — both readings are ancient.

---

### v.15 — "searchings of heart" vs. "great indecision"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "great were the searchings of heart" | chiqq'qei lev — "searchings/deliberations of heart" |
| LOGOS | "there were great searchings of heart" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "there was great indecision" | Interprets "searchings" as indecision |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew chiqq'qei lev (from chaqaq, "to inscribe/decree/search") paired with lev ("heart") conveys internal deliberation — the clans of Reuben debating with themselves whether to join the battle. "Searchings of heart" preserves the Hebrew metaphor. PNEUMA's "indecision" captures the result but loses the imagery and reduces a rich phrase to a flat abstraction.

**Status:** Minor. Imagery vs. interpretation.

---

### v.25 — "curds" vs. "yogurt"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "curds" | chem'ah — thickened milk product |
| LOGOS | "curds" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "yogurt" | Modern equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew chem'ah refers to a thickened, curdled milk product — closer to modern "curds" or "butter" than to yogurt. "Yogurt" is anachronistic in connotation (a modern processed product). "Curds" is the standard scholarly rendering and fits the ancient nomadic context.

**Status:** Minor. Lexical precision.

---

### v.30 — "A womb or two" vs. "a woman or two"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a womb, two wombs for the head of each man" | racham rachamatayim — literal |
| LOGOS | "A womb or two for every warrior" | Literal with slight smoothing |
| PNEUMA | "a woman or two for every warrior" | Replaces "womb" with "woman" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew racham means "womb" — it is a deliberate, dehumanizing synecdoche. Sisera's mother and her princesses imagine captured women reduced to their reproductive function. PNEUMA's "a woman or two" sanitizes the text and removes the very feature that makes the line so disturbing. The poet chose "womb" for a reason: to expose the casual brutality of the imagined victors. The consensus must preserve this.

**Status:** Major. The dehumanizing language is essential to the poem's moral vision.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental Hebrew ambiguity | 1 | biphroa p'ra'ot (v.2) — leaders vs. locks of hair |
| PNEUMA replaces imagery with interpretation | 2 | "Searchings of heart" → "indecision" (v.15), "womb" → "woman" (v.30) |
| PNEUMA substitutes meaning | 1 | "Villagers ceased" → "no warriors" (v.7) |
| Qere/ketiv variant | 1 | 1st vs. 2nd person in v.7 |
| Minor lexical modernization | 1 | "Curds" → "yogurt" (v.25) |

**Overall pattern:** The Song of Deborah pushes the three translators to their limits. EMET excels here — its literal approach captures the poem's archaic force, especially in vv.26-27 and v.30. PNEUMA's dynamic approach smooths away too much: replacing "womb" with "woman" (v.30) and "searchings of heart" with "indecision" (v.15) domesticates a poem that is wild, archaic, and deliberately unsettling. The most significant disagreement is v.2 — an ambiguity that has resisted resolution for three millennia.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
